title: Paper Explained: COMIC: Agentic Sketch Comedy Generation - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: AI creates funny sketches with viewer feedback

content.background:
Before this work, making funny short videos with AI was still a big hurdle. Sketch comedy is a team sport: writers, directors, actors, editors, and many others bounce ideas around to shape jokes, timing, and characters. AI tools mostly acted on their own, producing one-off lines or generic content that felt flat, repetitive, or out of touch with real movie-goer humor. In short, there wasn’t a good way for AI to imitate the collaborative, iterative process of a writers’ room, which meant the jokes often missed the mark or didn’t feel fresh.

A second big problem was figuring out whether a joke lands. Humor is highly subjective and context-dependent, and what makes one audience laugh often doesn’t work for another. Measuring “funniness” with simple rules is unreliable, and relying on a few human judges is expensive and slow. The paper points to using a large collection of comedy videos from YouTube to guide what people actually find funny, but turning that messy, real-world data into a reliable guide for automated systems is not straightforward. This gap—how to evaluate humor at scale and tailor it to real audience tastes—made it hard for AI-made sketches to feel engaging or to improve over time.

All of this adds up to a clear motivation: researchers want AI to help create funny, diverse, and high-quality sketches without requiring endless human labor, while making sure the output matches what real viewers actually enjoy. In a world where online video content is abundant but quality and relevance vary wildly, there’s a strong need for systems that can imitate the teamwork of a writers’ room and learn from real audience feedback so the humor stays fresh, relatable, and scalable.
content.methodology:
Here’s the core idea in beginner-friendly terms, with the key steps and the big innovation kept simple and concrete.

1) What they built (the overall approach)
- They created an automated system that can generate short comedic videos in a style similar to sketch shows like SNL.
- The system starts with character seeds or references and then uses a team of virtual agents that mimic real production roles (like writers, producers, actors, and editors).
- These agents work in an iterative cycle of idea generation, competition, evaluation, and improvement to produce high-quality and diverse sketches.

2) How it works, step by step (conceptual, no math)
- Seed characters: You give a few character references (quirks, attitudes, catchphrases). Think of this as setting up the cast and vibe for the show.
- Population of roles: A crowd of virtual agents acts like a writers’ room, a director’s team, and an editing crew. Each agent has tasks typical of that role—brainstorming lines, shaping scenes, directing how a scene should feel, cutting it down, etc.
- Generate and compete: The agents propose multiple sketch ideas or scenes. Like a friendly competition in a writers’ room, the best ideas move forward while others are revised or dropped.
- Automatic humor evaluation: Instead of relying on humans, the system uses LLM “critics” trained to judge humor. These critics are guided by analyzing a large set of actual comedy videos from YouTube to learn what audiences tend to laugh at.
- Iterative improvement: The top ideas are refined through more rounds of generation and critique, continually balancing novelty and humor until the system settles on strong scripts and story flows.
- Produce the video: The final scripts and directions are then turned into a short video using a video-generation pipeline, producing a complete sketch ready to watch.

3) The big innovation (why this is new and interesting)
- LLM critics aligned to real viewer tastes: Instead of hand-tuning jokes or relying on generic humor rules, the system’s humor evaluators (the critics) are trained to reflect how real audiences respond, by learning from a large library of YouTube comedy. This helps the AI judge what’s actually funny to people, not just what seems clever to a machine.
- An AI “production studio” with competition: Rather than a single model generating content, they use a population of agents that imitate a real studio’s workflow. The agents compete, critique, and improve each other’s ideas, which encourages richer diversity and higher quality over multiple passes.
- End-to-end automation for content creation: From character seeds to a finished video, the process is designed to run with minimal human intervention, showing how AI can orchestrate creative workflows that combine writing, performance, and editing.

4) Why it matters and what it means in practice
- The results aim to reach professional sketch quality and push the frontier of automated video generation, showing that an AI-driven pipeline can handle both funny ideas and the production logic behind sketches.
- The approach gives a practical blueprint for automated, audience-aligned content creation: seed-based character setup, a roles-based agent system, audience-informed evaluation, and iterative refinement leading to final media output.
- It also comes with caveats worth noting: humor is subjective and culturally nuanced, so what’s funny to one audience may not be to another; real-world deployment would need ongoing alignment with diverse viewer tastes and ethical considerations around content.
content.results:
COMIC presents a fully automated system that can create short comedic videos in the style of sketch shows like SNL. The key idea is to run a little “production team” inside the computer: many small AI agents take on roles inspired by real studio people (writers, editors, directors, performers, etc.). These agents compete, critique, and improve ideas over multiple rounds, so the best jokes and best video ideas rise to the top. A standout part of the system is its humor evaluator: an LLM-based critic that is aligned with actual viewer tastes by analyzing a large collection of comedy videos from YouTube. In short, the system doesn’t just spit out random clips; it tries to produce funny, engaging sketches that real people would enjoy.

Compared to earlier AI efforts, COMIC goes beyond single-model or one-off script generation. Past approaches often produced either scripts or videos in isolation, with limited feedback and creativity. COMIC combines end-to-end production with a multi-agent “production team” and an audience-aware humor evaluator, enabling more diverse ideas and higher-quality results. The combination of a collaborative agent system and audience-aligned feedback leads to sketches that feel more polished and entertaining, and it pushes the quality of automatically generated video closer to what professional studios deliver.

The practical impact is quite big. If such systems continue to improve, they could help creators brainstorm ideas, prototype sketches quickly, or produce lighter-weight content without large teams or expensive shoots. It could also serve as a learning tool for students and aspiring writers to study how humor is built and turned into a video, by showing how different roles contribute to a finished piece. Overall, COMIC represents a significant step toward end-to-end, automated video comedy that is not only technically impressive but also aligned with what real viewers find funny.
content.significance:
COMIC matters today because it shows how to automate an entire creative pipeline for comedy, not just a single AI task. It uses a population of agents that mimic real production roles (writer, director, editor, etc.) and runs an iterative loop of idea generation, evaluation, and improvement. The twist is LLM critics that are aligned with audience humor preferences by analyzing a large set of YouTube sketches. This combination of multiple specialized agents and audience-aligned evaluation helps produce higher-quality, more diverse sketches without human hands-on editing every step. In a moment when video content is exploding online, this approach demonstrates that AI can take big creative tasks from concept to short video.

This work influenced later developments in several ways. It helped popularize the idea of multi-agent AI pipelines where different models take on distinct production roles and continually refine results through competition and feedback. It also foreshadowed the rise of LLM-based evaluators and preference-driven learning that we see today in systems like ChatGPT and related tools, which use feedback and alignment to steer outputs toward human tastes. In the industry, you can see echoes of these ideas in AI video creation and editing platforms (for example, Gen-2-style tools from Runway and other AI-assisted video studios) that combine scripting, planning, generation, and automated critique to accelerate content production.

The long-term significance is that COMIC helps us think of AI as a collaborative production studio rather than a single-output generator. Its emphasis on role-based agents, iterative evaluation, and audience-aligned humor feeds into modern AI practices used by people everyday (LLMs as copilots, evaluators, and assistive editors). This paves the way for scalable, personalized media creation—where humor, style, and pacing can be tuned to different audiences—while highlighting important considerations about bias, copyright, and responsible use. In short, COMIC offers a blueprint for how creative AI could scale up, diversify, and stay aligned with human preferences, influencing the future of AI-powered entertainment and beyond.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding LLM Critics: The Heart of COMIC
concept_explanation.content:
Imagine a talent show where a panel of judges watches dozens of short sketches and scores them on how funny, surprising, and well-timed they are. In the COMIC system, those judges are called LLM Critics. They aren’t real people, but large language models that act like reviewers who assess the humor in each sketch. The judges get their sense of what audiences find funny by studying a huge collection of comedy videos from YouTube and the ways people react to them (likes, comments, shares, and even the tone of the comments). The idea is to train the critics to align with real viewer preferences so the system can produce sketches that people actually enjoy.

Here’s how it works step by step, in simple terms. First, the system gathers data about humor from YouTube comedy videos: what kinds of jokes land, how timing and setup lead to funny moments, what topics or characters tend to work, and how viewers react. Second, the LLM Critics are prompted or fine-tuned to evaluate humor along common dimensions like joke quality, timing, originality, and audience fit. They’re not just judging one script; they’re comparing many possible versions and saying which ones feel funnier or sharper to viewers. Third, a team of AI agents—each taking on roles similar to people behind the scenes (writer, producer, editor, etc.)—creatively proposes sketches and edits. The Critics score these proposals, and the system uses those scores to select the best candidates or to guide further revisions.

A concrete example helps. Suppose the agent team comes up with three different versions of a coffee-shop sketch. Version A has a fast, snappy punchline but relies on a common stereotype. Version B adds a witty dialogue twist but slows the pace. Version C uses a clever misdirection and a character quirk that feels fresh. The LLM Critics read these options and rate them based on humor quality and audience resonance learned from YouTube data. The system then picks Version C to develop further or suggests targeted tweaks to Version B’s pacing. This feedback loop keeps improving the sketches without needing a human writer to manually test every idea.

Why is this idea important? Because it helps AI-generated comedy stay engaging and relevant to real audiences at scale. Traditional humor research is labor-intensive and subjective; by grounding critics in large-scale viewer data, COMIC attempts to mirror what many people find funny, not just what a single designer thinks is funny. This approach can speed up production, reduce costs, and enable creators to tailor content to different audiences or platforms. Of course, it also raises concerns about bias or stereotypes in humor, so developers must monitor the critiques and ensure the system promotes inclusive, respectful comedy while still aiming to be entertaining.

In terms of practical use, LLM Critics could be a valuable tool for studios, online content creators, or education projects that explore humor. They can help auto-generate and refine short sketches, adapt jokes to specific demographics (e.g., college students vs. general audiences), or assist in rapid iteration during a live-writing process. Beyond sketch comedy, the idea extends to any scenario where you want AI-generated content shaped by human preferences—like humorous ads, interactive stories, or training materials that need to land with a target audience—while keeping the development cycle fast and scalable.

summary: This paper introduces a multi-agent system that automatically generates comedy sketches and uses LLM critics trained on YouTube humor to evaluate jokes, achieving near-professional video quality and state-of-the-art AI sketch generation.
